data.source: Large-scale online survey of Italian participants rating internet jokes (Covid-19 related and non-Covid) on funniness and aversiveness
data.wave_or_subset: Data collected March 18–30, 2020; analyses based on 1751 native Italian speakers aged 18+
data.notes: Participants also provided demographics, Coping Humor Scale (CHS), Life Orientation Test-Revised (LOTR), perceived infection risk, spatial distance from the epicenter, and social distance (known cases among acquaintances). Ratings used a 7-point scale coded 0–6. Items included Covid verbal jokes, memes, strips, and non-Covid verbal jokes. Random effects modeled for Participants, Items, and Province.

method.description: The study presented Italian participants with Covid-19-related and non-Covid internet jokes and asked them to rate funniness and aversiveness. Participants also completed CHS and LOTR scales and reported perceived infection risk, geographic proximity to the outbreak epicenter, and social distance to Covid-19 cases. Data were analyzed with linear mixed-effects models in R (lme4), using crossed random effects for participants, items, and province, with hierarchical model building and likelihood ratio tests, and continuous predictors z-centered.
steps: Select internet jokes from humorous Facebook pages, categorizing Covid-19 jokes into verbal, memes, and strips; include non-Covid verbal jokes as baseline.
Recruit participants online (social media, email) in Italy during March 18–30, 2020; screen for native Italian speakers aged 18+.
Collect demographics and administer CHS and LOTR questionnaires.
Measure psychological distance: perceived infection risk, spatial distance from the epicenter, and social distance (known Covid-19 cases among friends/relatives).
Present jokes and collect funniness and aversiveness ratings on a 0–6 scale.
Compute geographic/spatial distance metrics; prepare item- and participant-level datasets.
Z-center continuous predictors; define contrasts for humor types (e.g., Covid verbal vs non-Covid verbal; meme vs Covid verbal; strip vs Covid verbal).
Fit hierarchical linear mixed-effects models with crossed random effects for Participants, Items, and Province.
Compare models using likelihood ratio tests and information criteria (AIC/BIC); interpret fixed effects and interactions.
Visualize and summarize estimated effects and by-item variability.
method.models: Linear Mixed-Effects Models (R lme4) with crossed random effects for Participants, Items, and Province; hierarchical model building with likelihood ratio tests; main contrasts among humor types; continuous predictors z-centered; model fit assessed via AIC/BIC.
method.outcome_variable: Funniness rating; Aversiveness rating (both on 0–6 scales)
method.independent_variables: Humor type (Non-Covid verbal, Covid verbal, Covid meme, Covid strip); Coping Humor Scale (CHS); Life Orientation Test-Revised (LOTR); perceived infection risk; spatial/geographic distance from outbreak epicenter; social distance (known cases among acquaintances); demographics (age, gender, education).

results.summary: Covid-19 humor was rated more aversive than non-Covid humor. Memes were found funnier than Covid verbal jokes, while Covid strips were rated as most aversive and less funny. Individuals with higher Coping Humor Scale scores found Covid-19 humor funnier and less aversive; dispositional optimism (LOTR) positively related to funniness. Perceived infection risk increased aversiveness, particularly for strips, and greater geographic distance from the epicenter was associated with higher humor appreciation.

numerical_results.unit: scale points per SD of CHS (β coefficient)
scale points per SD of CHS (β coefficient)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Aversiveness difference (Covid verbal minus Non-Covid verbal)
